signals: Autonomic state is mostly stable: HRV generally sits inside your balanced baseline, with a few short dips that rebound quickly.
Sleep quantity is broadly adequate (~7–7.5 h average), but recovery quality is occasionally limited by fragmentation (awake time/restlessness spikes).
Sleep/resting heart rate is trending slightly downward from mid-January into early March (favorable adaptation signal), with brief spikes on poor/strained nights.
Daily stress load is moderate overall with a handful of high-stress days; early March looks lower-stress than late Jan/Feb.

Your physiology appears to respond more reliably to multi-signal patterns (HRV + sleep fragmentation + RHR + stress) than to any single metric alone (e.g., one short-sleep night didn’t necessarily drop HRV).
evidence: HRV baseline (undated summary): balanced range 43–54; weekly_avg 50; last_night_avg 45; low_upper 41.
Monthly HRV drift (approx.): Jan 12–31 avg ~47.9; Feb (available nights) avg ~46.2; Mar 1–9 avg ~50.4 (best stretch of the period).
Sleep duration: Jan avg ~7.45 h; Feb avg ~7.41 h (excluding missing 2026-02-07, 2026-02-23); Mar 1–9 avg ~7.20 h. Extremes: 4.16 h on 2026-01-16 (sleep score 50) and 9.45 h on 2026-02-01.
Sleep fragmentation/quality flags: max awake 1.28 h on 2026-03-08 (sleep score 73, restless 69) and high awake 0.73 h on 2026-01-31 (sleep score 72, HRV 37).
Sleep RHR trend (sleep resting HR): Jan avg ~46.8 bpm → Feb avg ~45.5 bpm → Mar 1–9 avg ~44.8 bpm. Extremes: 52 bpm on 2026-01-15; 40 bpm on 2026-02-28 and 2026-03-01.
Stress (avg): Jan avg ~22.9; Feb avg ~23.7; Mar 1–9 avg ~21.6. Highest stress avg 44 on 2026-02-07 (sleep missing). Lowest stress avg 15 on 2026-02-23 (sleep missing) and 2026-03-02.
Key “strain signature” day: 2026-01-31 combined HRV 37 + stress avg 35 + awake 0.73 h + sleep score 72 + sleep RHR 49 bpm (multi-signal suppression).
implications: Overall: your internal recovery system looks resilient and adaptive—most stress appears to be absorbed without sustained HRV suppression, and early March shows your best autonomic profile (HRV in low/mid-50s with low sleep RHR).

The most meaningful recovery limiter in this window is *sleep continuity* (awake time/restlessness), not consistently short sleep duration. When sleep is fragmented (e.g., 2026-03-08), HRV can drift down over the next 24–48 hours even if total sleep time is decent.

Use a combined “yellow flag” heuristic: (HRV near/below the low end of balanced) + (sleep RHR elevated vs your usual) + (sleep score <~75 or awake time notably high) + (stress avg elevated). This combo—rather than any one metric—best identifies your true low-readiness days (e.g., 2026-01-31).

The downward drift in sleep RHR from Jan→Mar suggests positive aerobic/autonomic adaptation or improved recovery efficiency; preserving that trend likely depends on avoiding clusters of disrupted sleep or prolonged high stress.

Hydration/fueling cannot be confidently tied to recovery here: hydration intake is recorded as 0.0 on all non-null days (likely logging/device artifact). Weight decreased from 82.42 (2026-01-12) to 79.38 (2026-03-07); if unintentional or aggressive, low energy availability could become a future HRV/sleep quality risk even if not yet obvious in this slice.
uncertainty: Two full sleep-data gaps: 2026-02-07 and 2026-02-23 (both have stress data only). 2026-02-07 is especially important because it had the highest stress avg (44) and could conceal a significant recovery disruption.
HRV units are not provided, and the HRV baseline block is undated—baseline range may have shifted during the period, so interpret ‘balanced’ thresholds cautiously.
Hydration intake values are non-physiologic (0.0 on every non-null day), so dehydration-related inferences are not reliable.
No VO2max data and sparse weight points limit triangulation between autonomic recovery and broader adaptation/energy status.

signals: Absorptive capacity appears solid: low-HRV episodes are usually brief (1–2 nights) and often followed by quick rebound into the 50s.
Resilience is good but not “bulletproof”: the biggest crash-risk pattern is a cluster of sleep fragmentation + high stress leading to HRV suppression (seen most clearly around 2026-01-31).
Longer-term adaptation signal is favorable: sleep RHR is drifting lower into March, and early March shows the best HRV stretch of the timeline.
Stress exposure is intermittently high (peaks and occasional high daily averages), suggesting that non-training stress could periodically limit how much training stress you can *effectively* absorb.
evidence: Fast rebound example: HRV hit its minimum 37 on 2026-01-31, then reached 53 by 2026-02-03 (within ~3 days).
Low-HRV occurrences (<43) were relatively infrequent: 7 of 55 available nights (~13%). Most were isolated singles or pairs rather than prolonged runs.
Notable low-HRV clusters/pairs: 2026-01-30→01-31 (41, 37); 2026-02-06→02-08 (40, missing sleep on 02-07, then 42); 2026-02-14 and 2026-02-16 (both 42) with rebounds on 02-15 (52) and 02-17 (54).
Sleep RHR monthly averages improved: ~46.8 bpm (Jan) → ~45.5 (Feb) → ~44.8 (Mar 1–9).

Stress avg monthly averages were stable-moderate (low 20s) with spikes: highest avg 44 on 2026-02-07; additional high-stress days include 2026-01-14 (34), 2026-01-31 (35), 2026-02-05 (32), 2026-02-11 (35).
implications: Macro-level constraint: you can likely handle progressive base rebuilding *when sleep continuity stays stable*, but your system shows clear vulnerability to sleep fragmentation + high stress; plan to preserve flexibility around periods likely to disrupt sleep (travel, late nights, unusual schedules) because those are the scenarios most likely to reduce absorptive capacity.

Robustness indicator to watch across 12–24 weeks: trend of sleep RHR. A sustained upward drift (e.g., +3–5 bpm above your recent average for several nights) paired with HRV at/below 43 is a strong sign your recovery margin is shrinking, even if subjective fatigue is mild.

Because HRV dips are usually short and followed by rebound, your season is less about chronic overreaching risk and more about avoiding repeated ‘hits’ before you fully rebound (stacking stressors before HRV/RHR normalize).

Weight loss across the period could become a medium-term recovery limiter if it reflects low energy availability; monitor for a future pattern of ‘adequate sleep time but worsening HRV + higher RHR + lower sleep scores,’ which can be consistent with under-fueling or cumulative stress.
uncertainty: Cannot separate exercise-induced ‘stress max’ from psychological/occupational stress without context; stress peaks (e.g., max 98 on 2026-03-06) may not represent harmful strain if they were activity-related.
Hydration logging is not usable (intake zeros), so you lack a reliable lever for diagnosing HRV/sleep fluctuations driven by dehydration.
Baseline HRV thresholds are undated; if baseline is moving upward with fitness, the same absolute HRV could represent different readiness states later in the season.

signals: Current day (Mon 2026-03-09) looks like *moderate readiness*: HRV 45 (within baseline but below weekly avg), sleep 7.32 h with score 82, stress avg 18 (low), stress max 70 (low).
Short-term caution: HRV has drifted down over the last 48 hours (57 → 48 → 45 from 2026-03-07 to 2026-03-09) following a highly fragmented night on 2026-03-08 (awake 1.28 h).
Sleep RHR is not elevated in the last few days, suggesting the HRV dip is more consistent with acute sleep disruption/cumulative strain than a strong illness/overreach signal.
evidence: HRV: 2026-03-07 = 57 (period max), 2026-03-08 = 48, 2026-03-09 = 45. HRV baseline balanced range (summary) = 43–54; weekly_avg (summary) = 50; last_night_avg (summary) = 45.
Sleep fragmentation spike: 2026-03-08 awake 1.28 h (period max), sleep score 73, restless 69 (high).
Sleep RHR last 3 nights: 47 (2026-03-07), 45 (2026-03-08), 47 (2026-03-09) — stable and not showing an upward drift.
Stress last 3 days: avg 25 (2026-03-07), 23 (2026-03-08), 18 (2026-03-09); stress max fell to 70 on 2026-03-09 (period minimum).
implications: GREEN (go): HRV ≥46 *and* within 43–54, sleep score ≥80, awake time not unusually high (<~0.6 h), sleep RHR ≤47, stress avg ≤25. This reflects adequate parasympathetic tone and low allostatic load.

YELLOW (caution): HRV 41–45 (like 2026-03-09) or a downward drift for 2+ nights *especially* after fragmented sleep (awake ≥~0.7 h, like 2026-03-08), even if stress avg is low. Use this as a limiter on intensity/complexity until HRV rebounds toward ~50 and sleep continuity normalizes.

RED (hold): HRV <41 (below ‘low_upper’ 41) OR 2 consecutive nights <43 combined with sleep score <75 and sleep RHR ≥49 and/or stress avg ≥30. This is the pattern most consistent with sympathetic dominance and reduced recovery capacity (best historical analog here: 2026-01-31).

Operationally for the next 7–10 days: prioritize actions that restore sleep continuity (earlier wind-down, reduce late stimulants/alcohol, temperature/light control). One ‘good-quality’ night often restores your HRV quickly; if HRV stays 43–45 for >3 nights despite adequate sleep time, assume accumulated fatigue and keep the week conservative until you see a rebound.

Race runway note: the 2026-03-28 10k trail race is 19 days after 2026-03-09; the key physiological constraint to absorbing any sharpening is avoiding clusters of fragmented sleep (your HRV responds quickly to that stressor).
uncertainty: Because the HRV baseline summary is undated, interpret absolute thresholds with a rolling 7-day personal mean as a secondary check (your summary weekly_avg is 50, which makes 45 meaningfully ‘below your current normal’ even if still ‘balanced’).
Two missing sleep nights in the historical window reduce confidence in identifying all stress→sleep→HRV patterns.
Stress max can be driven by exercise bouts; without session context, use stress *avg* + nighttime HRV/RHR as the primary readiness signal.
